coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for build_manifest edit ownership boundary — it reads like a one-way door.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py: 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 & 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (or an explicit merge step) as the owner of the final combined build_manifest function incorporating both the phase/floor lines (5967) and the pins parameter (acaf), with the parent's canonical ordering verified by a manifest-ordering golden test.
evidence: acaf: 'Extend build_manifest(..., *, pins: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] = ()) so absent pins preserve current manifest bytes exactly'
5967: 'Restructure build_manifest's fixed initial list to emit exact lines in this order after advisory: and before rebar-version:'
acaf specifies pin line position as 'after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines'; 5967 specifies review-phase/priority-floor position as 'after advisory: and before rebar-version:'
Neither child references the other's changes to build_manifest or designates which story writes the final combined function
findings.finding: Both acaf and 5967 independently describe adding lines to build_manifest without documenting a coordination plan or ownership boundary for their overlapping edits to the same function. Acaf says 'Extend build_manifest(..., *, pins: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] = ())' and adds pin lines in a specific manifest position; 5967 says 'Restructure build_manifest's fixed initial list to emit exact lines in this order after advisory: and before rebar-version: review-phase: planning|execution, then for execution only priority-floor: 0.80.' Both children modify build_manifest's line ordering and neither names the other's changes to that function, risking a merge conflict or incorrect final manifest ordering on the feature branch.
findings.location: child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c, Implementation section (build_manifest); child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, Implementation section (build_manifest)
scenarios: If acaf lands first, it adds the pins= parameter; when 5967 later restructures the fixed initial list, it must not regress acaf's pin-line placement or the pins parameter

If 5967 lands first, it restructures the fixed list; when acaf later adds pins=, it must append after verified-at-sha as specified — but 5967's restructuring may have changed the relative position of verified-at-sha
A merge conflict in build_manifest could silently produce a manifest ordering that differs from the parent's specified canonical order, causing signature mismatches
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cross-reference in both acaf and 5967 naming the other's build_manifest change and specifying that the combined function must be integration-tested against the parent's full canonical manifest line order. Alternatively, designate acaf as the earlier landing story for the pins parameter and require 5967 to produce a combined build_manifest that includes both changes atomically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate acaf as the sole owner of extending sidecar.emit/build_payload with reviewed_related_material, and rewrite 75de's Implementation section to say 'sidecar.emit gains the reviewed_related_material keyword-only parameter as delivered by acaf; 75de adds only parse_reviewed_related_material and the health-derivation consumer.'
evidence: acaf Implementation: 'In _run_plan_review, retain child acaf's pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot as local review_snapshot. On the full LLM path call sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root). Extend sidecar.emit and build_payload with keyword-only reviewed_related_material: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None'

75de Implementation: 'In _run_plan_review, retain child acaf's pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot as local review_snapshot. On the full LLM path call sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root). Extend sidecar.emit and build_payload with keyword-only reviewed_related_material: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None; build_payload serializes it as an ordered list of exact {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} items.'
The text is nearly word-for-word identical in both children with no 'as defined by acaf' or 'this is acaf's code' qualifier in 75de's section
findings.finding: Both acaf and 75de describe editing _run_plan_review to thread reviewed_related_material through sidecar.emit, without a clear boundary for which child writes the production code. Acaf says 'In _run_plan_review, retain child acaf's pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot as local review_snapshot. On the full LLM path call sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root). Extend sidecar.emit and build_payload with keyword-only reviewed_related_material.' 75de's Implementation section contains nearly identical language: 'In _run_plan_review, retain child acaf's pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot as local review_snapshot. On the full LLM path call sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root). Extend sidecar.emit and build_payload with keyword-only reviewed_related_material.' The same edit appears attributed to both children, creating a scope overlap where either child could write the code, leading to a gap (if each assumes the other did it) or a conflict (if both write it).
findings.location: child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c, Implementation section (_run_plan_review / sidecar.emit); child 75de-02b0-d454-410b, Implementation section (_run_plan_review / sidecar.emit)
scenarios: If 75de is implemented before acaf: 75de writes the sidecar.emit extension; when acaf later tries to 'extend sidecar.emit,' it finds the code already present — or worse, writes a conflicting version
If acaf is implemented before 75de: acaf writes the extension; 75de re-describes the same edit without realizing it's already done, causing confusion about which test suite covers the behavior
If neither child writes it assuming the other will, the feature is missing in both delivered stories
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the sidecar.emit/build_payload extension language from 75de's Implementation and replace it with a reference: 'Consume acaf's reviewed_related_material field via sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material; acaf owns emission.' Acaf's Implementation retains ownership of the emit-side change. Add a cross-story acceptance criterion to 75de: '[ ] sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material consumes the field emitted by acaf's build_payload extension.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the Problem statement's invalidation claim to be conditional on enforcement being enabled, or add a sentence clarifying that invalidation is gated by the enforcement flag, so the Problem and Enforcement sections are consistent.
evidence: Problem: 'material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation'
Enforcement: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false. Pin drift remains visible but advisory while disabled; valid remains true'
findings.finding: The Problem section states 'material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation' unconditionally, but the Enforcement section specifies that invalidation only occurs when enforce_plan_material_pins=true; with it disabled, drift is advisory and valid remains true. An implementer reading the Problem first and the Enforcement section second faces contradictory statements about whether drift invalidates attestations.
findings.location: Problem section ('material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation') vs. Enforcement section ('Pin drift remains visible but advisory while disabled; valid remains true')
scenarios: An implementer might implement unconditional invalidation on drift, misreading the Problem as the always-on behavior.
A reader onboarding to the codebase may believe drift always invalidates, leading to incorrect incident triage.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Problem statement to: 'When enforcement is enabled, material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation; when disabled, drift is recorded and visible but advisory.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that singleton prerequisite escalation starts from the model that failed the context-limit (i.e. uses `models_at_or_above(current_model)`, mirroring the existing `pass1_with_ladder` pattern) rather than restarting from `cfg.model`.
evidence: Plan: 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively. A singleton escalates through models_at_or_above(); if it still exceeds the largest window, emit too-large remediation.'

The base case 'singleton' is handled, but the intermediate case where halving produces a bin of size 1 that STILL hits a context limit at the current model (not yet the largest) is ambiguous: does it immediately escalate models, or keep halving (which would produce empty bins)?
The existing pass1_with_ladder halves by criterion, never by prerequisite bin, so there is no existing code pattern to reference.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a prerequisite context-limit error 'splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively,' but provides no fallback rule for the decision path when the recursion reaches an empty or zero-length half — which can occur if a single prerequisite is larger than half the budget but smaller than the full window.
findings.location: Settled design › Prerequisite consistency review — 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively.'
scenarios: A prerequisite bin of 2 items hits context limit → splits to [item1] and [item2] → [item1] alone also hits context limit at the current model → plan says 'singleton escalates through models_at_or_above()' but the plan also says the base case for a singleton-that-exceeds-the-largest-window is too-large; the intermediate model-escalation step for singletons is not a decision-rule gap since 'singleton escalates' is stated, but the rule for which model the escalation starts from (the current bin's model vs. cfg.model) is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline the consumer list (claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, derived health) directly into AC #7 so the criterion is self-contained.
evidence: AC text: 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift.'
Consumer list appears only in Settled Design / Enforcement: 'claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health' — not repeated in the AC.
findings.finding: AC #7 uses the phrase 'every named consumer' without listing the consumers in the AC itself, requiring cross-reference to the Settled Design section to know what must be tested; an agent could unknowingly miss a consumer (e.g. re-sign or drift-refresh) when writing the enforcement test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift.'
scenarios: An agent writes enforcement tests covering claim and close but misses drift-refresh or re-sign because those names don't appear in the AC.
A future AC reader can't evaluate completeness without knowing the canonical consumer list.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'every named consumer' with the explicit enumeration: 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates claim, close, attestation reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived-health evaluation after related material drift.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Bind AC #15 to at least one concrete, testable output surface (e.g. 'Sub-floor execution findings appear in the detailed review output and are labeled with their below-floor priority score').
evidence: AC text: 'Sub-floor execution findings remain visible.'
Output surfaces are listed only in Health and documentation section: 'detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI.'
Absence: no surface is specified in the AC itself.
findings.finding: AC #15 uses 'visible' without binding it to a specific output surface, making the criterion subjective and hard to verify in-session without cross-referencing the documentation section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Sub-floor execution findings remain visible.'
scenarios: An agent writing a test for this AC doesn't know which output to assert against — a CLI snapshot test, an MCP response schema check, or something else.
The criterion could be satisfied trivially by claiming findings appear in internal logs rather than a user-facing surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'Sub-floor execution findings appear in the detailed plan-review output (CLI show and MCP ticket detail) with their computed priority score, even when that score is below 0.80.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gold baseline protocol for AC #20: state how the one-per-call baseline is produced (e.g. committed fixture files generated from a known test set), where it lives in the repo, and how the CI gate compares packed vs. baseline results.
evidence: AC text: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract.'

Settled Design: 'release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero.'
Absence: no protocol for generating, committing, or refreshing the gold baseline is described; 'tail-item fixtures are mandatory' appears but without specifying fixture format or location.
findings.finding: AC #20 ('Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract') cannot be evaluated in-session because the plan does not specify how or where the one-per-call gold baseline is generated and stored — an agent cannot write a passing gate test without this protocol.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract.' and Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review — release-gate paragraph.
scenarios: An agent implementing this writes a test that generates a fresh one-per-call baseline at test time, which may be nondeterministic for LLM-backed calls and never actually gates the release.
Two agents disagree on whether the baseline should be pre-committed or computed on-the-fly, producing incompatible CI configurations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the prerequisite batching section: 'The gold baseline is a committed set of per-prerequisite coverage records produced by the one-per-call path against a fixed test fixture corpus; the CI gate runs the packed path against the same corpus and compares recall, false-acceptance rate, and attribution-error count against the committed baseline records.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs covering ManifestFormatError for the new parsers: at minimum one AC for malformed/duplicate pin lines, one for the priority-floor-without-review-phase rule, and one for invalid numeric floor values.
evidence: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines.'
'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError'
ABSENCE: No AC addresses parser error cases for manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins().
findings.finding: The plan specifies detailed ManifestFormatError behavior for the new parsers (duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, invalid numeric lines, and the priority-floor-without-review-phase rule) but no AC verifies that these error paths exist or are triggered correctly. This is a substantial behavioral deliverable with no acceptance coverage.
findings.location: Settled Design / Signed plan-material pins section: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines' and 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError'; Acceptance Criteria (all 22 items)
scenarios: A parser silently ignoring a malformed pin line would pass all 22 ACs but violate the spec, allowing corrupt attestation metadata to be treated as valid.
The priority-floor-without-review-phase rule, if unimplemented, could allow malformed execution attestations to pass as planning reviews.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that prerequisite material is never included in general finder or verifier calls (e.g., by confirming the specialized criterion runs in isolated calls with separate prompts).
evidence: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material.'
ABSENCE: No AC verifies that prerequisite material is excluded from general finder/verifier calls.
findings.finding: The plan specifies an isolation constraint — 'General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material' — which is a correctness/security boundary ensuring prerequisite evaluation stays in isolated prompts. No AC verifies this isolation is maintained.
findings.location: Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review section: 'General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material'
scenarios: If prerequisite material leaks into general finder calls, it could generate findings attributed to the wrong criterion or contaminate the standard review with relationship context the spec explicitly excludes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC#20 (or add a companion AC) to verify the fallback-to-one-per-call behavior when packed evaluation fails the fidelity gate, and confirm that tail-item fixtures are required.
evidence: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory. Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing.'
AC#20: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract.'
findings.finding: The plan describes a release gate and fallback for packed prerequisite evaluation ('Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing') and mandatory tail-item fixtures, but AC#20 ('Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract') covers only the positive fidelity case and does not verify the fallback behavior or the tail-item fixture requirement.
findings.location: Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review: 'Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory.' vs. AC#20
scenarios: If packed evaluation degrades below the fidelity threshold and the fallback is not implemented, the system ships incorrect packed behavior with no AC catching it.
Missing tail-item fixtures leave edge cases untested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add as an explicit prerequisite step) that the current manifest parser ignores unrecognized line types; if it does not, specify an expand-contract sequence — first deploy the lenient parser, then deploy the pin-writing code — and add this to the rollback/deployment plan.
evidence: 'an older verifier authenticates that stored list including unknown pin lines, then its field-specific parsers ignore lines they do not consume'
ABSENCE: no statement that the current parser already ignores unknown lines, nor an expand-contract step to make it do so before new pin lines are written
findings.finding: The plan asserts that older manifest readers 'ignore lines they do not consume' and that 'additive pin lines do not alter signature reproduction', but it does not confirm that the *current* manifest parser implementation already has this property, nor does it provide an expand-contract sequence if the existing parser raises ManifestFormatError on unrecognized line types. If the current parser is strict about unknown lines, adding plan-material-pin lines to new attestations would silently break any code path that reads those attestations with the old parser.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'Backward verification does not reconstruct a manifest from recognized fields... unknown additive lines do not alter signature reproduction'
scenarios: A deployed instance with the old parser reads a new attestation containing plan-material-pin lines; if the parser raises ManifestFormatError on unknown tokens, every new attestation becomes unreadable until all readers are upgraded — a silent breaking change with no rollback path described for this specific sub-case.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'Signed plan-material pins' or 'Enforcement and rollout' section: 'An explicit prerequisite is that the manifest parser already ignores unrecognized line types (or a preparatory release adds this leniency before pin lines are written). CI must include a cross-version test: an old-parser build reads a pin-bearing attestation and must not raise.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit documentation targets for `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`, and the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` / `TargetPinDetail` schemas — either extend the named 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' to cover them or call out a separate operator/API reference section.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation'
ABSENCE: no documentation target named for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close, or DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema

'Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI' — multiple external surfaces consuming an undocumented contract
findings.finding: The new configuration flags `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`, and the new `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` API contract returned by compute_validity (consumed by CLI, MCP, audit, close errors, and web UI), lack explicit documentation targets in the plan. The plan assigns only `plan-material-pin` syntax to 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' but does not specify where operators will learn about the new flags or where MCP/UI consumers will find the health contract schema.
findings.location: Settled design / Health and documentation; Settled design / Enforcement and rollout
scenarios: Operators enabling enforcement find no reference documentation for the two flags.
MCP or web UI implementers must reverse-engineer the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema from source rather than a documented contract.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Health and documentation' section, add: 'The two enforcement config flags and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth/TargetPinDetail contract are documented in the same advanced attestation/gate documentation, with the operator configuration reference cross-linking the flags and their defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight validation note for the 0.80 execution floor — e.g., a stated intent to measure false-block/pass-through rates on early rebar reviews, or a reference to prior calibration data that informed this value, so the policy basis is auditable and recalibration is grounded.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, measurement study, or pilot metric is mentioned to validate or calibrate this threshold.
Absence: no 'TBD: measure X' or 'derive from fixture' language for the floor value.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-review priority floor is asserted as a fixed protocol policy with no measurement plan or empirical basis cited; even a policy threshold whose value may need revision in practice benefits from a stated validation or calibration step.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: The 0.80 floor proves too aggressive, blocking legitimate execution-phase reviews and creating re-review churn with no data to guide recalibration.
The floor proves too lenient for the actual risk profile of in-progress work, undermining the policy intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable to produce an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the rationale for: (1) the signed pin manifest format and backward-compat strategy, (2) natural vs. eager propagation, and (3) the outside/inside-lock revision-comparison transaction protocol.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'
Absence: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or architectural design doc for the pin manifest format, natural-propagation model, or outside/inside-lock transaction pattern.

The plan explicitly justifies design choices (e.g., natural vs. eager propagation, no new storage primitive, additive-metadata backward-compat) that are prime ADR content but are embedded in the plan prose rather than captured in a durable artifact.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — the signed plan-material pin format, the natural (non-eager) propagation model, the outside-lock/inside-lock revision-comparison transaction pattern, and the plan_review_phase compiled-state projection — but names only user-facing 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' rather than an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or internal design document capturing the rationale for these decisions.
findings.location: Settled design / Health and documentation — 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'
scenarios: A future maintainer changes the propagation model or the manifest line ordering without realising these were deliberate, architecturally constrained choices, introducing subtle compatibility breaks.
The outside/inside-lock retry protocol is misunderstood during a refactor and the revision-comparison invariant is broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the prerequisite semantic-consistency criterion as an explicit named goal in the Outcome section so the title and stated outcomes faithfully cover all deliverables in the plan body.
evidence: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material.'
'The criterion receives the subject plan and the current narrow material of each prerequisite'
Title: 'Keep plan-review attestations current across relationships and execution' — does not mention new LLM-based semantic consistency review

ABSENCE: No explicit statement in Problem or Outcome sections that semantic prerequisite consistency is a goal; Outcome section only describes 'signed plan-material pins' and 'material drift invalidation'
findings.finding: The prerequisite semantic-consistency LLM review (a new specialized criterion that evaluates whether the subject plan is logically consistent with each prerequisite) goes meaningfully beyond the title's promise of 'keeping attestations current across relationships'. The title implies staleness detection (fingerprint drift); the body also introduces substantive new review capability (semantic consistency evaluation). An implementing agent might treat this as in-scope only because it is bundled here, while reviewers expecting only currency-tracking may miss the implications of the new LLM criterion.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review; Acceptance Criteria lines 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' and 'Every upheld prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical ID'
scenarios: An agent implementing only the fingerprint-pinning portion could claim the epic complete, having skipped the substantial prerequisite-consistency LLM criterion work, because the title and Outcome section do not surface that goal.
A reviewer comparing scope to title may flag the LLM criterion as out-of-scope, causing unnecessary churn during review.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Outcome section to include: 'A new prerequisite-consistency criterion evaluates whether the subject plan is logically consistent with each direct prerequisite's current material, producing a three-disposition coverage record per prerequisite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC or documented validation step that provides a concrete before/after workflow comparison or dogfooding scenario showing the feature prevents an agent from claiming/closing a ticket whose prerequisite plan has materially changed.
evidence: Problem: 'an agent can therefore claim or close work using assumptions that no longer match the plans it relies on'

ABSENCE: No AC or health criterion specifies a before/after scenario, a measured reduction in stale-attestation-driven claim/close events, or a dogfooding step in the rebar project that demonstrates the feature preventing an actual incorrect close.
'Rebar enables both opt-in enforcement flags while reusable defaults remain disabled' — the closest to a real-world validation, but describes configuration, not outcome measurement.
findings.finding: The plan lacks a concrete validation mechanism — a before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding step — that would confirm the feature actually reduces stale-assumption-based agent errors in practice. The ACs are structural/contractual (pins are signed, drift is detected, flags work) but none verify the end-to-end value claim that agents no longer act on outdated plan assumptions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section; Problem statement
scenarios: The feature ships with all structural ACs passing but never demonstrably prevents a stale-attestation-driven agent error, leaving the core value claim unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'In the rebar project with enforcement enabled, an agent attempting to close a ticket after its prerequisite description changes receives a pin-drift blocking error rather than proceeding to close.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that 'inbound blocks preload' means the existing `find_inbound_relationships` approach (byte-scan + reduce-mentioning-candidates) run outside the lock, and confirm that the retry loop's tracker-HEAD-change detection is sufficient to handle new inbound edges arriving between retry iterations.
evidence: Plan text: 'One preload supplies inbound blocks discovery without per-target store reductions.'
find_inbound_relationships in reducer/_inbound.py reduces every mentioning candidate ticket to confirm the relationship against structured state
A byte-scan without reduction could produce false positives (incidental mentions) and miss resolved aliases — meaning relationship pins could be based on an incomplete prerequisite set
findings.finding: The plan says 'One preload supplies inbound blocks discovery without per-target store reductions' but the only existing mechanism for inbound-blocks discovery (`find_inbound_relationships`) performs a byte-scan PLUS reduces every mentioning ticket. Under the plan's pre-sign retry loop this entire O(scan+reduce-N) operation must execute outside the lock on each retry; but if 'without per-target store reductions' means the plan intends a lighter path (e.g., only scanning event-file bytes without reducing), it will miss relationship membership changes that only become visible after reduction (e.g., a LINK event that has landed but whose resolved target_id appears in the candidate's compiled state). The plan needs to clarify whether 'preload' means the existing reduce-mentioning-candidates approach (O(scan+reduce-N), correct but expensive on each retry) or a lighter byte-only scan (faster but potentially misses resolved aliases).
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'One preload supplies inbound blocks discovery without per-target store reductions.'
scenarios: A ticket's ID appears as a prose mention in a comment on another ticket; a byte-only scan includes it as a prerequisite; the plan is pinned to a non-relationship, inflating pin counts and possibly blocking review.
A depends_on link uses an alias that byte-scans as the canonical ID; without reduction the alias is not resolved, and the inbound relationship is missed, leaving a prerequisite unpinned.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'The inbound-blocks preload uses find_inbound_relationships (byte-scan + reduce-mentioning candidates) outside the write lock; its cost is paid once per retry iteration. Under the lock, only the tracker HEAD SHA is re-checked — not the full reduction — so the retry loop bounds the repeated work to at most three outside-lock passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that every manifest-building path (build_manifest, drift-refresh/resign) must co-emit review-phase: and priority-floor: as an atomic pair when either is present, and that the manifest builder enforces this invariant structurally (not just the parser).
evidence: Plan text: 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError'
Plan text: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'

resign_plan_review in attest/resign.py reconstructs a manifest from the prior one and re-signs it; the plan does not address whether resign preserves or re-derives the review-phase and priority-floor lines
If resign omits the phase line while copying the floor line from an execution attestation, the result is a malformed attestation that compute_validity immediately rejects
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the manifest order inserts `review-phase:` and optional `priority-floor:` lines between the existing `advisory:` line and the existing `rebar-version:` line, but also states that `manifest_review_phase()` returns `planning` when the phase line is absent (legacy attestations). The `compute_validity` plan-review branch then parses all three new fields together inside one ManifestFormatError catch. However, the plan does not specify the behavior of `compute_validity` when it encounters a manifest that has a `priority-floor:` line but a MISSING `review-phase:` line on an ALREADY-SIGNED attestation that arrived via re-sign. The plan states 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' — but re-sign paths (`resign_plan_review`) reconstruct a new manifest from the old one and may add phase/floor without validating the source manifest. If re-sign produces a floor-without-phase manifest, compute_validity would return malformed/invalid for every re-signed attestation.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'a priority-floor line without a review-phase line raises ManifestFormatError' and Settled design / Execution reviews: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'
scenarios: An execution-phase attestation undergoes drift-refresh (resign path); the prior manifest carries priority-floor: 0.80 but the resign code does not copy or re-derive review-phase:; the refreshed manifest is floor-without-phase and compute_validity returns malformed, blocking claim.
A developer writing the resign path forgets to add review-phase before priority-floor because the invariant is only stated for the parser, not for the manifest builder.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the manifest spec: 'build_manifest and all re-signing paths (drift-refresh, resign) must emit review-phase: before priority-floor: as an atomic pair; the builder raises if floor is requested without a phase argument, mirroring the parser's ManifestFormatError.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why prerequisite bin context-overflow uses recursive contiguous-halves splitting rather than the established `pass1_with_ladder` per-item + model-escalation pattern, or restate the design to reuse that existing ladder.
evidence: Plan text: 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively. A singleton escalates through models_at_or_above(); if it still exceeds the largest window, emit too-large remediation.'
Existing codebase pattern in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py pass1_with_ladder(): batch → one-criterion-per-call → models_at_or_above() escalation — no binary halving.

Since each evaluation covers exactly one prerequisite, halving a multi-prerequisite batch eventually degenerates to per-item calls, making the recursive halving equivalent to the existing ladder but with more recursion overhead and no codebase precedent.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a prerequisite bin exceeding the context limit 'splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively' — a binary halving strategy that does not exist in the codebase and diverges from the established size-handling ladder (pass1_with_ladder: batch → one-per-item → model escalation). The plan does not justify why this new recursive-halving approach is preferred over simply applying the existing per-item fallback ladder, nor validate that it is meaningfully different when each evaluation already covers exactly one prerequisite.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review: 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively.'
scenarios: A reviewer implementing this feature looks for the halving primitive and finds none; they must build it from scratch without understanding why it was chosen over the established ladder.

A bin of N prerequisites that all exceed the per-item window would recurse all the way to singletons anyway, making the halving equivalent to the existing one-per-call path but with O(log N) extra failed attempts.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the recursive-halving description with: 'A provider context-limit error falls back to one-prerequisite-per-call using the existing `pass1_with_ladder` / `models_at_or_above()` escalation pattern; if a single prerequisite still exceeds the largest window, emit too-large remediation.' This reuses proven code and eliminates a novel pattern with no prior art in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure disposition when the old-reducer compaction compatibility test fails (e.g., release is blocked), and add a DATA LOSS RISK note with a compensating-forward strategy (e.g., re-derive phase on next upgrade from raw history rather than only from compiled status) for the case where old compaction strips `plan_review_phase` despite the assertion.
evidence: Plan asserts: 'compaction drops only its explicit strip set, so an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it'
Plan specifies a compatibility test but gives no failure disposition: if the test fails, is the release blocked? Is there a compensating-forward path?

POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: plan asserts phase survives compaction rollback but only under the condition that old compaction does not strip it; the plan does not specify what the state looks like if that condition is violated
ABSENCE: no DATA LOSS RISK note or fallback strategy for the case where old reducer compaction strips plan_review_phase
findings.finding: The rollback schema state for `plan_review_phase` in compaction snapshots depends on a property of the OLD reducer codebase — that its compaction strip-set does not include `plan_review_phase` — but the plan specifies no failure disposition if the compatibility test reveals the old reducer DOES strip the key, and no compensating-forward strategy for that scenario. If old compaction silently drops `plan_review_phase` after rollback, tickets re-reduced by old code lose their phase state; on the subsequent re-upgrade, bootstrap re-derives phase from compiled status, which can yield an incorrect value for in-flight tickets (e.g., a ticket currently `blocked` that was correctly phase=`execution` becomes `planning` if its status-bootstrap rule maps `blocked` → `execution` only when status ≠ open/idea — this particular case is actually correct per the spec, but the silent loss path itself is unspecified).
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout: 'compaction drops only its explicit strip set, so an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction' / 'Compatibility tests run old reduction plus re-compaction against a phase-bearing snapshot'
scenarios: Old reducer's compaction does strip unknown keys (e.g., the strip set is a whitelist, not a blacklist); compatibility test catches this only if it is run; if gating is not enforced, the release ships with a silent phase-loss-on-rollback defect

Rollback is performed in an emergency without running compatibility tests; plan_review_phase silently vanishes from compacted snapshots; next upgrade must re-bootstrap all tickets from compiled status
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the rollback section: 'If compatibility tests show that old-reducer compaction strips plan_review_phase, the release is blocked until either (a) the old reducer is patched to pass through unknown keys, or (b) the re-upgrade bootstrap rule is extended to scan committed STATUS events rather than only compiled status, ensuring correct phase re-derivation without relying on snapshot persistence. DATA LOSS RISK: silent phase drop under old compaction cannot be recovered without re-scanning raw history.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and register a named structured-output contract (e.g. `plan_review_prerequisite`) for the PrerequisiteCoverageRecord, including the `disposition` enum Literal, the optional `reason_code` Literal, and the fail-safe coercion rule for each field.
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite. Its schema is {prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: evaluation-error|output-invalid|attribution-invalid, detail?: string}'
No contract name like 'plan_review_prerequisite' appears anywhere in the plan text.
Existing analogues: plan_review_findings, plan_review_completion, plan_review_novelty each have a named Pydantic factory + register_contract() call in passes.py lines 213–222.
No prompt file name (e.g. 'plan-review-prerequisite-finder') is identified, unlike PASS_FINDER, PASS_CONTAINER, PASS_ISF etc. which are all named constants.
findings.finding: The specialized prerequisite-consistency criterion introduces a new LLM call with a new output type (`PrerequisiteCoverageRecord`) whose `disposition` enum (consistent|finding|indeterminate) and `reason_code` enum (evaluation-error|output-invalid|attribution-invalid) are defined only in prose — no structured-output contract name (e.g. `plan_review_prerequisite`), no Pydantic model factory, and no `contracts.register_contract()` call is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Settled design › Prerequisite consistency review — Pass 1 schema paragraph
scenarios: An implementer must invent both the contract name and the Pydantic model shape without any plan-level guidance, risking schema drift between the LLM call and the consumer that builds PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.

The `reason_code` field is conditional (present only for indeterminate) — without a Pydantic model specifying Optional[Literal[...]] the verifier has no structured envelope to coerce bad values, unlike pass2_completion which uses explicit coerce helpers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_pass1_prerequisite_model()` factory producing a `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` Pydantic model with `disposition: Literal['consistent','finding','indeterminate']` and `reason_code: Optional[Literal['evaluation-error','output-invalid','attribution-invalid']] = None`, register it as `'plan_review_prerequisite'`, and name the prompt constant (e.g. `PASS_PREREQUISITE = 'plan-review-prerequisite-finder'`) in `passes.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify for `prerequisite_attribution_valid`: (a) which Pydantic class it lives in (Binary vs PlanSeverityAttrs), (b) that it is na-default (add to `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`), and (c) whether `prerequisite_id` on P1Finding is `Optional[str] = None` with pass-through in `pass1_chunk`'s output reshaping.
evidence: Plan: 'Prerequisite candidate findings add optional prerequisite_id to the existing finding schema.'
Plan: 'The shared index-keyed Pass 2 verification schema gains optional prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na; non-prerequisite findings use na.'
Existing `_pass1_model()` in passes.py lines 94–114 has no `prerequisite_id` field; `plan_review_verification_model()` in verify_models.py lines 183–261 has no `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field.

The plan does not say whether `prerequisite_attribution_valid` belongs in `Binary` (alongside other graded/veto sub-answers) or in `PlanSeverityAttrs`, nor whether it is na-default or insufficient-default.
Every existing na-default field is declared in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` frozenset (verify_models.py:82–88) — this set must be updated but the plan does not mention it.
findings.finding: The plan adds an optional `prerequisite_id` field to the existing P1 finding schema and a `prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na` field to the Pass 2 verification schema, but neither the extension mechanics for the concrete Pydantic models (`_pass1_model()` / `plan_review_verification_model()`) nor a na-default/coercion rule for `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is specified, leaving the schema mutation undefined.
findings.location: Settled design › Prerequisite consistency review — Pass 1 and Pass 2 paragraphs
scenarios: If `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is added to Binary with insufficient-default rather than na-default, it will drag validity scores for all non-prerequisite findings in every existing plan review.
If it is placed in PlanSeverityAttrs rather than Binary it will not participate in validity computation at all, making the attribution-check a no-op.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the execution-phase threshold formula to match the real `threshold_for` signature: specify that the 0.80 floor applies as `block_threshold = max(threshold_for(criteria)[0], 0.80)` inside the `_threshold_for` wrapper, preserving the `blocking_enabled` component unchanged.
evidence: Plan: 'normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) to blocking criteria'
src/rebar/llm/criteria/model.py:45: `def threshold_for(criteria: list, routing_map, *, gate) -> tuple[float, bool]`
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:700–701: `def _threshold_for(criteria: Any) -> tuple[float, bool]: return _criteria.threshold_for(criteria, crit_by_id, gate='plan_review')`
The existing caller wraps this tuple and passes it as `threshold_for=` to `pass3_over_findings` which unpacks it as `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = threshold_for(f.get('criteria', []))`
findings.finding: The plan's execution-phase block-threshold formula `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` uses the wrong signature: the real `threshold_for` takes a criteria LIST plus a routing map and returns a `(block_threshold: float, blocking_enabled: bool)` tuple, not a scalar; applying `max()` directly to the tuple would compare tuples, not extract the threshold.
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate — 'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan's pseudocode verbatim would write `max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` which compares a tuple to a float and raises TypeError at runtime.
The correct form is `max(threshold_for(criteria)[0], 0.80)` inside the lambda, replacing the `block_threshold` component of the returned tuple while preserving `blocking_enabled`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the formula with: 'During execution, the `_threshold_for` wrapper is modified to `thr, blocking = _criteria.threshold_for(criteria, crit_by_id, gate="plan_review"); return (max(thr, 0.80), blocking)` — applying the floor only to blocking criteria (i.e. when `blocking` is True).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add acaf as a declared dependency of 7c27 and correct the Upstream Contract to attribute PlanRelationSnapshot to acaf (not 75de).
evidence: 7c27 Upstream Contract: '75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text'

acaf Implementation: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py, which imports no signing or orchestration modules'

7c27 Implementation start check: 'verify both symbols and contract tests exist; if either differs, stop as upstream contract drift' — if 7c27 checks 75de for PlanRelationSnapshot, it will fail this precondition spuriously
75de only references PlanRelationSnapshot as 'child acaf's typed {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} record' — it is a consumer, not the owner
findings.finding: Child 7c27's Upstream Contract misattributes PlanRelationSnapshot ownership to 75de ('75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text'), but PlanRelationSnapshot is defined by acaf ('Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py'). Furthermore, acaf is absent from 7c27's declared dependency list (which lists only 75de and 5967), creating a dependency gap: if acaf has not yet landed on the feature branch when 7c27 is implemented, the PlanRelationSnapshot type and its contract tests will not exist where 7c27 expects them.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56, Upstream Contract section; child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c, Implementation section
scenarios: An implementer of 7c27 checks 75de's merged branch for PlanRelationSnapshot and cannot find it, triggering a false 'upstream contract drift' halt
If the implementation sequence places 75de before acaf, 7c27's upstream contract check fails even though both siblings are in scope
The 7c27 start-of-implementation upstream_contract test (run pytest -k upstream_contract) will fail or target the wrong module
findings.suggested_fix: In 7c27's Upstream Contract, replace '75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload' with 'acaf must deliver PlanRelationSnapshot (in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py) with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text; 75de must deliver pin-health enforcement and compute_validity extensions.' Add acaf to 7c27's declared dependency links alongside 75de and 5967.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion covers the critical rule that ANY indeterminate PrerequisiteCoverageRecord causes the whole review to emit unsigned INDETERMINATE rather than a signed PASS. AC 18 only tests that each readable prerequisite receives a coverage record; it does not test the whole-review disposition consequence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC); Settled Design > Signed plan-material pins: 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A review with one or more indeterminate prerequisite coverage records is emitted unsigned with disposition INDETERMINATE and cannot be used to satisfy the close gate.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
